name: Body Roll Anchor
description: Replace the triple step with a body roll that flows from chest to hips. Dramatic and sensual styling.
execution: ["Beat 5: Initiate roll from chest","Beat &: Roll passes through torso","Beat 6: Complete roll through hips, grounding into the floor","Keep feet planted — the movement is in the body","Arms maintain connection while body rolls","Don't force it — it should flow naturally"]
musicality: Perfect for slow, sultry music. R&B, blues, or any music with a strong groove. Use on dramatic moments.

name: Musical Anchor
description: Freestyle anchor that responds directly to what you hear in the music. No fixed footwork — pure interpretation.
execution: ["Listen to the music — what does it call for?","Match your movement to the specific sounds you hear","Could be a hit, a slide, a pause, a roll — anything","Maintain connection and stretch with partner","Stay grounded — don't lose the anchor function","The goal is to make the music visible through your body"]
musicality: For any music — this is about listening and responding. Best used when you really know a song or feel a strong musical impulse.

name: Sailing Anchor
description: Smooth, elongated anchor that glides through the beats. Like sailing through water — no sharp stops.
execution: ["Beat 5: Begin long step back with smooth transfer","Beat &: Glide through — no sharp step, just flow","Beat 6: Complete the smooth weight transfer","Everything is legato — connected and flowing","Think of pulling taffy — slow, smooth, continuous"]
musicality: Perfect for slow, smooth songs. R&B, ballads, or any music with sustained notes and long phrases.

name: Standard Anchor
description: The fundamental anchor step. Triple step in place with stretch and compression creating an elastic connection with your partner.
execution: ["Beat 5: Step back with right foot (follower) or left foot (leader), creating stretch","Beat &: Close with opposite foot","Beat 6: Step in place, settling into the connection","Maintain resistance — don't collapse toward partner","Arms should have tone but not rigidity"]
musicality: Use for everything — it's the default. Works with any tempo or mood. The bread and butter of WCS.

name: Stutter Anchor
description: Quick, choppy anchor with extra steps squeezed in. Adds rhythmic interest and energy to the end of a pattern.
execution: ["Beat 5: Quick step back","Beat &: Quick step (extra stutter)","Beat a: Another quick step (the stutter)","Beat 6: Final step — grounding","Keep it small — the extra steps are in place, not traveling","Stay grounded — the energy is rhythmic, not vertical"]
musicality: Best for uptempo songs with lots of rhythmic detail. Funk, pop, or when the music has staccato elements.

name: Syncopated Anchor
description: Plays with off-beat timing for the anchor steps. Creates rhythmic tension that resolves satisfyingly.
execution: ["Break the standard timing — delay or anticipate steps","Beat 5: Hold or delay the first step","Beat &: Sharp step on the off-beat","Beat 6: Resolve into the ground","The key is intentional timing shifts, not being late","Must be musical — the syncopation should match the music"]
musicality: For music with syncopated rhythms, jazz elements, or when you hear something interesting in the off-beats.
